fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up this native library integration thing we've been talking about. Basically we want devs to be able to pass strings into the native side and get them back without having to worry about all the low-level byte stuff themselves. The big pain point right now is that when devs do this manually they keep running into silent corruption issues or crashes on the native side — it's really hard to debug and it's been causing incidents. We want one clean operation that just handles it for them.

The structure should be similar to how we split things out in that JSON adapter pattern we used on the last boundary project — keep the wire format parsing totally separate from the actual transfer logic, same idea here. The native side expects things in a specific byte encoding and the managed runtime doesn't always play nice with that, especially with certain special characters (you know the ones — the emoji stuff and some edge case characters that the runtime technically allows but that break things downstream).

We need a test runner too, should write output files per case so we can diff them easily. The output format for each run needs to be consistent — show the operation, the result, and something that proves the value actually went through the encoding step rather than being passed through directly. Rejections should come back as clean category labels, no stack traces or runtime noise leaking into the output.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Strings must be transferred as UTF-8 byte sequences. The core boundary operation must encode the managed string to UTF-8 before passing it to the native side, and decode the returned UTF-8 bytes back to a managed string on return.

For a successful round-trip, the adapter must print exactly three lines: `op=take_string`, then `output=<the returned string>`, then `utf8_bytes=<number of UTF-8 bytes in the returned string>`, each terminated with a newline. The byte count is mandatory and proves the value crossed the boundary as encoded bytes.

When the boundary rejects a string (e.g. due to an unpaired surrogate), the adapter prints exactly two lines: `op=take_string` then `error=invalid_utf8`. No exception type, stack trace, or runtime message text may appear. The error category must be the language-neutral string `invalid_utf8`.

Any string that cannot be validly encoded as UTF-8 must be rejected at the boundary before any bytes are passed to the native side. The canonical example is a lone UTF-16 surrogate code point such as U+D800. Managed runtimes may permit such values in strings but they have no valid UTF-8 representation and must never reach native memory.

trigger_keywords: where does the JSON parsing go
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core boundary/marshalling logic must live in its own separate unit with no knowledge of JSON, stdin, or stdout. A distinct execution adapter unit handles reading the JSON command, invoking the core transfer operation, and formatting the output lines. Do not collapse everything into one file, and do not build a multi-layer framework — small and well-separated is the target.

trigger_keywords: what JSON does the adapter read
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter reads exactly one JSON object from stdin with the shape `{"op": "take_string", "value": "<string>"}`. The `op` field identifies the operation and the `value` field carries the string to transfer.

The test harness writes one file per case to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`. For example, the first case in `feature1_1_valid_string_roundtrip.json` run with the default `public_test_cases` cases-dir produces `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_valid_string_roundtrip@000.txt`. Each file contains only the raw stdout of the program under test — no PASS/FAIL metadata.

The single test entry point is `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` argument that points to a directory of case JSON files; the default is `public_test_cases`. Output is namespaced by the cases-dir value so different runs never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: how many bytes is a supplementary plane character
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A supplementary-plane character such as 💖 (U+1F496) is stored as a surrogate pair in UTF-16 managed runtimes but encodes to 4 bytes in UTF-8. The `utf8_bytes` field must reflect the actual UTF-8 byte length — 4 for that emoji — confirming it crossed the boundary as encoded bytes and was not short-circuited.
The empty string is a valid input. It must round-trip successfully and the adapter must print `op=take_string`, `output=` (empty), and `utf8_bytes=0`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'take_string'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that JSON adapter pattern we used on the last boundary project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Strict Separation of Concerns constraint in the PRD: the execution adapter is a separate unit responsible only for JSON parsing, invoking the core TakeString operation, and formatting neutral output lines — mirroring the adapter/core split described in the Architecture section of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: the emoji stuff and some edge case characters that the runtime technically allows but that break things downstream
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Unpaired UTF-16 surrogate code units (e.g. U+D800) which Go and other managed runtimes can store in strings but which have no valid UTF-8 representation; these must be caught at the boundary and reported as `error=invalid_utf8` per Feature 1.2 in start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: something that proves the value actually went through the encoding step rather than being passed through directly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The mandatory `utf8_bytes=<n>` third output line in the success case, which reports the UTF-8 byte length of the returned string and thereby proves the value was encoded/decoded as bytes rather than short-circuited as a managed string reference

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that specific point — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ to figure out the exact expectation. The test cases are the ground truth for what the output should look like.

feature1_1_valid_string_roundtrip.json.description: Send a Unicode string across the foreign-function boundary into the native string-transfer routine and receive it back unchanged. Valid strings of any plane must round-trip byte-for-byte; the reported UTF-8 byte length confirms the value crossed the boundary as encoded bytes rather than being short-circuited. Covers the empty string, a Basic-Multilingual-Plane character, and a supplementary-plane (surrogate-pair) character.

feature1_2_invalid_utf8_rejection.json.description: Send a string containing an unpaired UTF-16 surrogate code unit into the native string-transfer routine. Because the value cannot be encoded as valid UTF-8, the boundary rejects it and the adapter reports a neutral invalid-UTF-8 error category instead of round-tripping the value.
